---
title: "Realtine consensus modules to traits (experimental variants)"
format: html
date: "2026-05-07"
bibliography: ../../../refs-stat-anal.bib
execute:
  fig-format: png
  fig.dpi: 300
---

## Rationale

The aim is to relate consensus modules from WGCNA to experimental variants.
This can entail: ~~inbred line~~, day.

Built with R version `r getRversion()`

## R Preliminaries

Environment cleaning and set-up

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
library(WGCNA)

# The following setting is important, do not omit.
options(stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
```

## Load data

```{r load}
rm(list = ls())

# Imput data for analysis
load('../rdata-saved/wgcna/Consensus-dataInput-MAD025.RData')

# wgcna results
load('../rdata-saved/wgcna/cons13merged025.RDa')

# exprSize = checkSets(multiExpr)
nSets = exprSize$nSets
```

We have loaded the variables `multiExpr` and `Traits` containing the expression and trait data, respectively. Further, expression data dimensions are stored in `nGenes` and `nSamples`. The consensus network analysis results are
represented by the variables `consMEs`, `moduleLabels`, `moduleColors`, and `consTree`.

## Relating consensus modules to experimetal variables

:::{.callout-important}
Approach for categorical variables is described in [post](https://peterlangfelder.com/2018/11/25/working-with-categorical-variables/).
:::

<!-- ```{r binarize} -->
<!-- # Correlation is computed for each set separately so 'line' could NOT be included! -->
<!-- ## Binarization of 'day' -->
<!-- for (i in c(1, 2, 3)){ -->
<!-- daybin_vsall = binarizeCategoricalVariable(Traits[[i]]$data$day, includePairwise = FALSE, includeLevelVsAll = TRUE) -->
<!-- Traits[[i]]$data = cbind(Traits[[i]]$data, daybin_vsall) -->
<!-- } -->
<!-- ``` -->

```{r binarize all}
for (set in 1:nSets){
  daybin_vsall = as.data.frame(binarizeCategoricalVariable(Traits[[set]]$data$day, includePairwise = FALSE, includeLevelVsAll = TRUE))
  rownames(daybin_vsall) = rownames(Traits[[set]]$data)
  Traits[[set]]$data = daybin_vsall
}
```

```{r correlations}
# Set up variables to contain the module-trait correlations
moduleTraitCor = list()
moduleTraitPvalue = list()
# Calculate the correlations
for (set in 1:nSets)
{
  moduleTraitCor[[set]] = cor(consMEs[[set]]$data, Traits[[set]]$data, use = "p")
  moduleTraitPvalue[[set]] = corPvalueFisher(moduleTraitCor[[set]], exprSize$nSamples[set])
}
```

We now display the module-trait relationships using a color-coded table. We print the correlations and the corresponding p-values, and color-code the entris by the p-value significance.

```{r module-trait, fig.width=10, fig.height=6, dpi=300, dev='png'}
# Convert numerical labels to colors for labeling of modules in the plot
MEColors = labels2colors(as.numeric(substring(names(consMEs[[1]]$data), 3)))
MEColorNames = paste("ME", MEColors, sep="")
# Plot the module-trait relationship table for each set
for (set in 1:nSets){
  textMatrix = paste(signif(moduleTraitCor[[set]], 2), "\n(",
  signif(moduleTraitPvalue[[set]], 1), ")", sep = "")
  dim(textMatrix) = dim(moduleTraitCor[[set]])
  # sizeGrWindow(10,7)
  par(mar = c(6, 8.8, 3, 2.2))
  labeledHeatmap(Matrix = moduleTraitCor[[set]],
  xLabels = names(Traits[[set]]$data),
  yLabels = MEColorNames,
  ySymbols = MEColorNames,
  colorLabels = FALSE,
  colors = blueWhiteRed(50),
  textMatrix = textMatrix,
  setStdMargins = FALSE,
  cex.text = 0.5,
  zlim = c(-1,1),
  main = paste("Module--trait relationships in", setLabels[set]))
}
```

## References
